serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hamburger (approximately 130 g) consisting of a white burger bun, a grilled beef patty, pickled vegetables, mustard, and tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Hamburger — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hamburger (cr_id F05020055). All six core and secondary nutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Hamburger, white bun — GI analogous estimate based on white bread burger bun (GI ~61) per University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for a complete hamburger exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate component — a white wheat burger bun — which has a published GI of approximately 61 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Foster-Powell et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (61 × 28.85 × 1.30) / 100 ≈ 21.6 for a 130 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 510 mg per 100 g, contributed by processed bun, pickled vegetables, mustard, and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid hamburgers due to the high sodium load (510 mg/100 g); requesting no pickles and minimal condiments can reduce sodium by an estimated 150–200 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (28.85 g/100 g) from white burger bun with low dietary fibre (1.92 g/100 g), yielding a medium-to-high glycemic load of approximately 21.6 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit hamburger consumption due to the high glycemic load from the white bun; substituting a wholemeal bun or lettuce wrap and avoiding sugary condiments can meaningfully reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) from beef patty and estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from beef and processing, combined with high sodium (510 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume hamburgers only occasionally; the combination of saturated fat, estimated trans fat, and high sodium elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk, and choosing a leaner patty with no added condiments reduces this risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ground beef is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory; tomato (nightshade) may trigger solanine-related inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid hamburgers with beef patties due to high arachidonic acid and trans fat content; substituting a grilled chicken or fish patty and omitting tomato reduces the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A hamburger is a Western-origin sandwich consisting of a ground beef patty served inside a sliced white wheat burger bun, typically accompanied by pickled vegetables (gherkins), mustard, and tomato sauce (ketchup). The beef patty is usually grilled or pan-fried, yielding a browned, savoury exterior with a juicy interior. The white bun provides a soft, slightly sweet bread base. Per 100 g, the hamburger delivers 242 kcal, 13.46 g protein, 7.69 g fat, and 28.85 g carbohydrate, with 1.92 g dietary fibre and a notably high sodium content of 510 mg driven by the processed bun, pickles, mustard, and ketchup. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium) based on the white bun as the dominant carbohydrate source, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 21.6 per standard 130 g serving. Clinically, the hamburger is a high-concern food for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high refined carbohydrate, medium-high glycemic load), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid from beef, estimated trans fat). It is a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated and trans fat alongside high sodium. The hamburger is widely consumed as a fast food or casual dining item across Singapore, Malaysia, and globally, and is commonly available at fast food chains and hawker-adjacent Western food stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a hamburger?
Can I eat hamburger if I have diabetes?
What is the GI of a hamburger?
Is hamburger good for weight loss?
Can I eat burger with high blood pressure?
Is beef burger bad for cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020055) at confidence 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. All six reported nutrient fields are taken verbatim. Saturated fat, sugar, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous hamburger entries to complete secondary nutrient fields. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on the white bun component.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific HPB hamburger composite entry is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the level of this composite preparation as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g (2.8 g/100 g), sugar_g (4.5 g/100 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Fast foods, hamburger; single, large patty; with condiments and vegetables' (FDC ID 173960) as the closest approved-source analogue; these values are approximate and should be verified against a direct HPB breakdown when available. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat burger bun GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Foster-Powell et al., 2002). The input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are factually incorrect for a beef hamburger and have been removed. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' has been retained in meal_context as Western-style breakfast burgers are commercially available. The region_tag 'kl' from the input has been noted but the food is classified as Western origin; it is available across Malaysia and Singapore.

improvement_actions_taken: Removed factually incorrect dietary_tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from input — hamburger contains beef.
Added saturated_fat_g (2.8), sugar_g (4.5), and trans_fat_g (0.1) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogous entry to satisfy Q3.
Added GI (61, medium) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database based on white burger bun; calculated GL per 130 g serving as 21.6.
Replaced input 'diabetes' condition key with 'type2_diabetes' and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Q7 requirement.
Added cardiovascular_disease health context entry (not present in input) to satisfy Q7.
Populated ontology section with defensible FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes.
Documented all -1 fields and estimation sources in notes per G3 requirement.
Ensured all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array per Q8 consistency rule.
